title: Antoinette Brown Blackwell: America's First Woman Minister
description: This coloring page introduces children to Antoinette Brown Blackwell, the first woman ordained as a minister in the United States. Students will learn about this important historical figure while developing fine motor skills and creativity.
instructions: Color the picture of Antoinette Brown Blackwell and the scenes showing her important work. Read the learning facts with an adult and discuss why she was important in history.

content.image: A portrait of Antoinette Brown Blackwell in the center wearing 1800s-style clothing with her hair pulled back. Around her are smaller scenes showing her preaching at a pulpit, writing at a desk, and standing with other early women's rights supporters. The background includes a church building and books representing her writings.

elements.name: Antoinette Brown Blackwell
elements.description: Color her dress in dark colors typical of the 1800s, perhaps dark blue or brown

elements.name: Church pulpit
elements.description: Color the wooden pulpit brown and the Bible on it black

elements.name: Writing desk
elements.description: Color the desk brown and the papers white

elements.name: Books
elements.description: Color the books different colors to show she wrote many important things

elements.name: Church building
elements.description: Color the church with gray stones and a brown door

learningFacts: Antoinette Brown Blackwell lived in the 1800s when women had fewer rights than men
She became the first woman minister in America in 1853
She wrote books and gave speeches about equality
She helped change people's ideas about what jobs women could do
She was brave and spoke up when something wasn't fair
